points.text: Use a short client timeout (e.g., 1‑2 s) and let the server enforce its own longer timeout to avoid cascading retries.
Implement exponential backoff with random jitter for retries, and cap the max retry attempts (e.g., 3‑5).
Add a circuit‑breaker that opens after N consecutive failures to stop further calls until health recovers.
Make retries idempotent or use request IDs so duplicate processing does not cause side effects.
Monitor end‑to‑end latency and set timeout values based on the 95th‑percentile of observed response times.
On the network side, check TCP retransmission rates, packet loss, and queue depth on switches/routers during incidents.
Verify TCP keepalive and retransmission timeout (RTO) settings; tune RTO if the network shows high latency spikes.
Use exponential backoff with jitter for retries to avoid thundering‑herd load spikes.
Implement a circuit‑breaker that stops retries after a configurable failure threshold.
Set client‑side request timeout shorter than the service’s maximum processing time plus network latency margin.
Limit the maximum number of retries per request to prevent unbounded traffic amplification.
Configure TCP_USER_TIMEOUT (or SO_RCVTIMEO) to bound how long a TCP socket can remain in retransmission before aborting.
Monitor RTT and variance (e.g., via TCP_INFO) to adapt timeout values dynamically to current network conditions.
Check for elevated packet loss or congestion using netstat -s, ss -i, or tcpdump to correlate retransmissions with network health.
Inspect switch/router queue lengths and bufferbloat; use tools like mtr or ping‑pong latency to detect queuing delays.
Verify NIC duplex and speed settings; mismatches cause retransmissions and latency spikes.
Ensure firewalls or load balancers have appropriate idle‑timeout settings that match application timeout expectations.
